bugs.description: The backtracking search explores an exponential number of possibilities (up to ~2^n * n! states) without memoization or pruning, which can cause time limit exceeded for n=10 in worst‑case inputs.
The backtracking search explores an exponential number of possibilities (up to ~2^n * n! states) without memoization or pruning, which can cause time limit exceeded for n=10 in worst‑case inputs.
The code only contains an import statement and lacks any implementation of the solution logic, so it cannot read input, process test cases, or produce output.

reviews.raw_response: {"verdict": "FAIL", "bugs": [{"description": "The code only contains an import statement and lacks any implementation of the solution logic, so it cannot read input, process test cases, or produce output.", "line": 1, "severity": "critical", "triggering_input": "any input"}]}

bugs.description: The code only contains an import statement and lacks any implementation of the solution logic, so it cannot read input, process test cases, or produce output.

The algorithm generates candidates by iterating over all permutations of the input array and taking prefix sums, which is O(n!) time and memory. For n=10 this is extremely slow and will cause TLE or memory exhaustion.

The code uses itertools.combinations to select n values from candidates, which only generates subsets with distinct elements and never includes repeated values. The required b sequence may contain duplicate numbers, so valid solutions can be missed.

When the number of generated candidates is less than n, the code still iterates over combinations of size min(n, len(candidates)), producing tuples smaller than n. This leads to checking b sequences of incorrect length and can incorrectly answer NO.

The overall approach of generating candidate b values as prefix sums of permutations does not guarantee that a valid b (if one exists) will be among those candidates, making the algorithm logically incorrect.

The algorithm generates candidates by iterating over all permutations of the input array and taking prefix sums, which is O(n!) time and memory. For n=10 this is extremely slow and will cause TLE or memory exhaustion.

The code uses itertools.combinations to select n values from candidates, which only generates subsets with distinct elements and never includes repeated values. The required b sequence may contain duplicate numbers, so valid solutions can be missed.

When the number of generated candidates is less than n, the code still iterates over combinations of size min(n, len(candidates)), producing tuples smaller than n. This leads to checking b sequences of incorrect length and can incorrectly answer NO.

The overall approach of generating candidate b values as prefix sums of permutations does not guarantee that a valid b (if one exists) will be among those candidates, making the algorithm logically incorrect.
The algorithm arbitrarily truncates the generated candidate b-values to the first 200 elements, which may exclude the actual b-values needed to satisfy the condition, causing false negatives.

Exhaustively iterating over all combinations_with_replacement of size n from the candidate set is combinatorial (C(|candidates|+n-1, n)) and will cause time limit exceeded for n up to 10, even with a modest candidate set.

The candidate generation method (adding/subtracting a_i repeatedly starting from 0) does not guarantee that all possible b-values that could form a valid solution are reachable, so the algorithm may miss valid solutions even without the truncation step.
The algorithm arbitrarily truncates the generated candidate b-values to the first 200 elements, which may exclude the actual b-values needed to satisfy the condition, causing false negatives.

Exhaustively iterating over all combinations_with_replacement of size n from the candidate set is combinatorial (C(|candidates|+n-1, n)) and will cause time limit exceeded for n up to 10, even with a modest candidate set.

The candidate generation method (adding/subtracting a_i repeatedly starting from 0) does not guarantee that all possible b-values that could form a valid solution are reachable, so the algorithm may miss valid solutions even without the truncation step.

The algorithm enumerates all combinations of size n‑1 from the candidate set, which can be up to 3^n (~59049 for n=10). The number of combinations C(|candidates|, n‑1) is astronomically large and causes the program to time out for typical test cases (e.g., n=10). This makes the solution infeasible.

The algorithm enumerates all combinations of size n‑1 from the candidate set, which can be up to 3^n (~59049 for n=10). The number of combinations C(|candidates|, n‑1) is astronomically large and causes the program to time out for typical test cases (e.g., n=10). This makes the solution infeasible.

The recursive search explores an exponential number of candidate b sequences (branching factor up to 2*|b|, depth up to n). For n=10 the worst‑case number of recursive calls is roughly 2^{n-1}·(n-1)! ≈ 1.8·10^8, which will cause a time‑limit exceed on typical judges. The algorithm is not optimized with pruning or memoization, so it is not guaranteed to finish within the limits.

The recursive search explores an exponential number of candidate b sequences (branching factor up to 2*|b|, depth up to n). For n=10 the worst‑case number of recursive calls is roughly 2^{n-1}·(n-1)! ≈ 1.8·10^8, which will cause a time‑limit exceed on typical judges. The algorithm is not optimized with pruning or memoization, so it is not guaranteed to finish within the limits.

fixes.code:
from itertools import permutations

def solve():
    n = int(input())
    a = list(map(int, input().split()))
    
    if n == 1:
        print("YES" if a[0] == 0 else "NO")
        return
    
    # Generate candidate b values: 0 and all prefix sums of permutations of a
    candidates = set([0])
    for perm in permutations(a):
        s = 0
        for x in perm:
            s += x
            candidates.add(s)
    
    candidates = list(candidates)
    
    # Try all combinations of n values from candidates
    from itertools import combinations
    a_set = set(a)
    # Need multiset coverage
    a_list = sorted(a)
    
    for b_combo in combinations(candidates, min(n, len(candidates))):
        b = list(b_combo)
        diffs = set()
        for i in range(len(b)):
            for j in range(len(b)):
                diffs.add(b[i] - b[j])
        if all(x in diffs for x in a):
            print("YES")
            return
    
    print("NO")

t = int(input())
for _ in range(t):
    solve()

from itertools import combinations

def solve():
    n = int(input())
    a = list(map(int, input().split()))
    
    # Generate candidates: 0 plus all a_i and their partial sums
    candidates = set([0])
    for x in a:
        new = set()
        for c in candidates:
            new.add(c + x)
            new.add(c - x)
        candidates |= new
    
    candidates = sorted(candidates)
    a_set = a  # need all elements covered (as multiset? no, just all values present in diffs)
    
    # Try all combinations of size n from candidates
    # With n<=10 and smart candidate generation, candidates ~ 2^n but let's cap smartly
    # Fix first element as 0 to reduce search space
    zero_idx = candidates.index(0)
    
    for combo in combinations(range(len(candidates)), n - 1):
        b = [0] + [candidates[i] for i in combo]
        diffs = set()
        for i in range(len(b)):
            for j in range(len(b)):
                diffs.add(b[i] - b[j])
        if all(x in diffs for x in a):
            print("YES")
            return
    
    print("NO")

t = int(input())
for _ in range(t):
    solve()
